svg.text: • GNU grep: historically Boyer-Moore-Horspool; now Two-Way + SIMD memchr scan.
• ripgrep: SIMD-accelerated memchr + BM-style skip + verify; the de-facto fastest grep.
• glibc memmem / strstr: Two-Way algorithm (BM cousin with O(1) space, O(n) worst).
• Editors (vim, VS Code "Find"): BM-Horspool for plain literal search.
• Antivirus signature scanning: multi-pattern Aho-Corasick, fall back to BM for long sigs.
• Database LIKE '%pattern%': some engines use BM under the hood.
• Network IDS (Snort, Suricata): BM for content matches in payload inspection.
• Bad-character table size scales with alphabet σ — Unicode (σ ≈ 10⁶) needs hash map, not array.
• Worst case O(n·m) without Galil — adversarial input like "aaaaa...aaab" matching "aaaab".
• For very short patterns (m ≤ 4), preprocessing dominates — use plain memchr / SIMD scan.
• Cache-unfriendly random shifts: skipping forward kills sequential prefetcher on huge texts.
• Case-insensitive search: normalize once or build BM tables on folded alphabet — don't fold per-step.
Modern reality: SIMD beats algorithmic cleverness for needle < 16 bytes.
BM matters most for long patterns over large alphabets — exactly where it was designed to win.
